explicit_text: I was born in Burma, but I don't really remember much about it because I was just a baby when my family fled due to the war and Burmese soldiers attacking our village. My parents escaped from Burma to Thailand to find safety. Even though I was born there, my memories of Burma are very faint since I grew up mostly in Thailand.
implicit_text: I was born in a Southeast Asian country, but I don't really remember much about it because I was just a baby when my family fled due to the war and soldiers attacking our village. My parents escaped from that country to its neighbor to find safety. Even though I was born there, my memories of that place are very faint since I grew up mostly in the neighboring country.

explicit_text: I grew up in Thailand for about 15 years before moving to the United States. I lived in refugee camps there, specifically the Mila refugee camp, where life was tough but manageable. We had limited resources, no electricity for most, and schools that taught Thai and English but not Burmese. Thailand was where I spent most of my childhood and learned about the challenges of refugee life.
implicit_text: I grew up in a Southeast Asian country for about 15 years before moving to North America. I lived in refugee camps there, specifically one called Mila, where life was tough but manageable. We had limited resources, no electricity for most, and schools that taught the local language and English but not my native tongue. That country was where I spent most of my childhood and learned about the challenges of refugee life.

explicit_text: I lived in the Mila refugee camp in Thailand, which was one of several camps there. Life in Mila was difficult; houses were made of bamboo and leaves, and we had no electricity unless you were rich. The United Nations and Thai people sometimes brought food supplies like rice and fish paste to the camp. School was available but sometimes required small fees, and we learned Thai and English there.
implicit_text: I lived in a refugee camp in a Southeast Asian country, which was one of several camps there. Life in that camp was difficult; houses were made of bamboo and leaves, and we had no electricity unless you were rich. International organizations and local people sometimes brought food supplies like rice and fish paste to the camp. School was available but sometimes required small fees, and we learned the local language and English there.

explicit_text: I moved to the United States when I was about 15 years old through a resettlement program organized by the United Nations. Before coming here, I went through interviews, health checkups, and training about life in America, like how to call 911 and what to expect. Life here is very different from the camps—there's clean water, bathrooms, schools, and more opportunities. The U.S. became my new home where I could learn English and live more comfortably.
implicit_text: I moved to North America when I was about 15 years old through a resettlement program organized by an international organization. Before coming here, I went through interviews, health checkups, and training about life in this country, like how to call emergency services and what to expect. Life here is very different from the camps—there's clean water, bathrooms, schools, and more opportunities. This country became my new home where I could learn English and live more comfortably.

explicit_text: The United Nations played an important role in my life by coming to the refugee camps in Thailand and offering resettlement opportunities to countries like the United States, Canada, and Europe. They helped organize interviews and health screenings to determine eligibility for moving abroad. The UN also sometimes provided food aid to the camps, helping families like mine survive during difficult times.

explicit_text: When I was living in the refugee camp in Thailand, we didn't have access to the internet or social media like Facebook. It was only after moving to the United States that I started to learn about Facebook and how people use it to connect. Back in the camp, technology like Facebook was not part of our daily life because we lacked computers and Wi-Fi.

explicit_text: In the refugee camp, the phones we used were mostly Nokia phones, the old basic models without internet or social media. These phones were simple and used mainly for calling, often with prepaid SIM cards. We didn't have smartphones or Wi-Fi back then, so Nokia phones were our main way to communicate with family and friends.

We didn't have smartphones or Wi-Fi back then, so these phones were our main way to communicate with family and friends.

It was part of the process after leaving the refugee camp in Thailand and completing all the interviews and medical checkups. JFK Airport was the gateway to my new life in America before moving on to Syracuse.

explicit_text: After arriving in the United States, I settled in Syracuse, New York. This city became my new home where I started attending school and learning English. Syracuse offered a very different life compared to the refugee camps, with better facilities like clean water, bathrooms, and schools. It was here that I began to adjust to American life and culture.
